topic: An adult child moves back in with their parents
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of lemon polish and old paper hung heavy in the air of the living room. I traced the grain of the antique mahogany table with my fingertip, the surface cool beneath my touch. Every creak of the floorboards above seemed amplified in the sudden silence. I kept glancing toward the hallway, where my parents were ostensibly watching television, their postures rigid, their movements too deliberate. I tried to focus on the stack of books on the shelf, but my attention kept snagging on the shadow that stretched and contracted with the afternoon light. A knot tightened low in my stomach, a persistent, dull ache that refused to dissipate. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, the movement feeling clumsy and overly loud in the quiet space.

[story 2]

"Everything alright?" Mom asked, her voice pitched a little too high, as if testing the air. I forced a smile, the muscles in my face feeling strangely stiff. "Fine, Mom. Just settling in." I moved toward the kitchen, needing the noise of the refrigerator hum to drown out the silence of the house. As I reached for a glass of water, I noticed the framed photographs on the mantelpiece seemed to shift slightly when I wasn't looking directly at them. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I took a long sip of water, my hand trembling just enough that I had to grip the glass tightly. The air in the room felt thick, pressurized, like before a storm breaks.

[story 3]

I spent the evening in my room, ostensibly reading, but my eyes kept darting toward the closed door of the master bedroom. The silence of the house was oppressive, a blanket woven from unspoken things. When my father cleared his throat from the adjacent room, the sound resonated through the thin wall, a sharp punctuation mark in the stillness. I pulled my knees to my chest, wishing the floor would swallow me whole. Every time the television volume was turned up slightly, the low murmur of conversation from the other room seemed to sharpen into something distinctly pointed. I found myself counting the patterns in the wallpaper, an obsessive, pointless task designed to keep my mind from drifting toward the periphery of what was happening.
